name: Ring corridor layout
description: A continuous corridor forms a closed rectangular or square loop around a small inner core, with rooms attached to both its outer perimeter and its inner face so that occupants can walk a full circuit and return to any starting point without backtracking. The inner ring typically contains one large hall, a service core (lifts, toilets, stairs) or a single specialised room, while the outer ring is subdivided into many smaller rooms whose facades face outward to daylight. This layout is the organising idea of modern museums, exhibition galleries, hospital nursing wards and circular office floors, where continuous circulation, equal access to every room and a strong centre are all required at once. The corridor stays 2 blocks wide along the full loop and never dead-ends.
placement_rules: Corridor must close into a continuous loop with no dead-end; every corridor cell has exactly two neighbouring corridor cells along the ring.
Inner ring boundary should sit at least 2 blocks inside the outer wall so the outer rooms have habitable depth; below this the outer rooms become unusably shallow.

Inner core occupies the geometric centre and contains either a single large room (atrium, central hall) or a tight cluster of service rooms (stair, lift, toilets); avoid subdividing the inner core into more than 2-3 rooms.
Corridor width stays uniform at 2 blocks along the entire ring; widening on one side breaks the symmetry that justifies the ring.
Outer rooms attach to the outer face of the corridor and reach the building's exterior wall, so each one gets its own facade window.
Total footprint should be roughly square (aspect ratio between 1:1 and 1:1.3); strongly elongated rectangles distort the ring into a racetrack and read as a double-loaded corridor instead.
Provide at least one door from the corridor ring into the inner core; otherwise the central space is unreachable.
Place vertical circulation (stair, ladder) inside the inner core, not interrupting the ring, so the loop reads as continuous on every floor.
lighting_ventilation: The outer rooms each receive a window on the exterior facade and provide ambient daylight to their stretch of corridor through open doorways; the inner core is interior unless capped by a skylight or open atrium. Supplement corridor lighting with sea_lantern or glowstone ceiling fixtures every 3-4 blocks around the full loop, and add a stronger central fixture (lantern cluster or shroomlight) at the centre of the inner core when it is enclosed.

modern.notes: Museum or hospital ring with flush white walls, smooth stone floor and recessed sea-lantern ceiling fixtures every few blocks around the loop.

industrial.notes: Converted-factory office ring with exposed iron trim, gray concrete walls and polished andesite floor; inner core often left as a full-height atrium.

minimalist.notes: Gallery-style ring in monochrome quartz with no visible trim breaks, relying on uniform lighting and the closed loop itself as the spatial gesture.
